The sqrt variant again requires a graph of infinite chromatic number whose subgraphs are near-bipartite within sqrt(n) edge deletions.
The problem is the infinitary version of chromatic number: a graph with chromatic number an infinite cardinal must contain an equal-chromatic-number subgraph.
The problem asks for a graph of chromatic number aleph_1 (G.chromaticCardinal = ℵ_1) on aleph_1 vertices.
The problem asks for a graph G of infinite chromatic number (G.chromaticNumber = ⊤) with large independent sets in every subgraph.
The statement asserts J(2*k, k).chromaticNumber = k + 1 for some k > 2, so the chromatic number is the central quantity.
Docstring asks for a graph with aleph_2 vertices and 'chromatic number aleph_2' whose small subgraphs have chromatic number at most aleph_0.
Erdos-Hajnal [ErHa68b] result: graphs of chromatic number aleph_1 with aleph_k vertices whose smaller subgraphs have chromatic number at most aleph_0.
f_k(n) is defined as 'the maximum possible chromatic number of a graph with n vertices which contains no K_k'.
Rodl's theorem: if G has chromatic number >= f(k) then G contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number >= k.
Asks for graphs of chromatic number $k$ (G.IsCritical k) in which every vertex is critical yet every critical edge set has size $>r$; problem tags include 'chromatic number'.
Reed's conjecture bounds the chromatic number chi(G) by ceil((omega(G)+Delta(G)+1)/2) per the docstring.
The finite-graph variant states the same Reed bound chi(G) <= ceil((omega(G)+Delta(G)+1)/2) on the chromatic number.
F n is the smallest order of a triangle-free graph with chromatic number n and f = 3; the theorem states F 3 = 7.

Module header 'Idoneal numbers completeness conjecture' asserts the list of 65 idoneal numbers is complete, referencing Wikipedia's Idoneal number page; no Wikidata item exists for the completeness conjecture itself.
Docstring: 'Hilbert's fifth problem (Gleason-Montgomery-Zippin, 1952): every locally Euclidean topological group is a Lie group.'

The greedy Sidon sequence A = {1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 21, 31, 45, …} of the docstring is the Mian–Chowla sequence (OEIS A005282, listed for problem 340 on erdosproblems.com).
Docstring: Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] asked whether 22 is in the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence, which it is.
Docstring: 33 is the smallest integer not known to be in the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence.
Docstring: it may be true that almost all integers (here: outside a set of density zero) are in A − A for the greedy Sidon sequence.
Docstring: it may be true that all or almost all integers (here: all but finitely many) are in A − A for the greedy Sidon sequence.
Asks for the exact order of growth of the greedy Sidon (Mian–Chowla, OEIS A005282) sequence's counting function.
Docstring: Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] asked whether the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence has positive density.

k and l are defined as 'the 2-adic valuation' and 'the 3-adic valuation' (padicValNat) of n(n+1), and the limsup question is about 2^k 3^l.

f(n) counts solutions to k*sigma(k)=n where sigma(k) is the sum of divisors of k (ArithmeticFunction.sigma 1).

Counts ordered pairs (a,b) with sigma(a)+sigma(b)=sigma(a+b), sigma being the sum of divisors function.
Docstring: 'Here sigma(n) is the sum of divisors function', the summand of the series sum sigma(n)/2^n.
The condition sigma(n)>cn uses the sum-of-divisors function (Lean: ArithmeticFunction.sigma 1).
The statement equates phi(n) with the sum-of-divisors function sigma(m) (σ 1 m via ArithmeticFunction.sigma).
The hypothesis sigma(n) > Cn uses the sum-of-divisors function (ArithmeticFunction.sigma 1) and asks when n is a distinct sum of its proper divisors.
Membership in A56777 requires sigma(n+12) = sigma(n) + 12, formalized with the sigma-1 arithmetic function.
A063880 lists numbers with sigma(n) = 2 * usigma(n), where sigma(n) is the sum of all divisors (module docstring).
The conjectured limit of A(n+1)/A(n) is (1 + sqrt 5)/2, the golden ratio.
Conjectures completeness for all t > 0 and 1 < alpha < (1+sqrt 5)/2, the golden ratio threshold.
The hypotheses hmup/hmuq require mu to be an invariant measure for T_p and T_q (MeasurePreserving (Tn p) mu mu and MeasurePreserving (Tn q) mu mu).
Conjecture 1.4 quantifies over atomless T_p-invariant Borel probability measures (MeasurePreserving (Tn p) mu mu).
Erdős Problem 20 is the sunflower conjecture — is f(n,k) < c_k^n for the k-sunflower threshold — with the file header citing Wikipedia's Sunflower (mathematics) and [ErRa60].
Docstring: Erdős and Rado [ErRa60] proved the factorial upper bound (k-1)^n n! for the k-sunflower threshold (the sunflower lemma).

The problem ('weak sunflower problem' per erdosproblems.com/857) asks for the minimal family size forcing a k-sunflower (IsSunflower) among subsets of [n].
The theorem is set over K = F((t^{-1})), the field of formal Laurent series, via polyToLaurent : F[X] →+* F⸨X⸩.
Docstring: 'The set of irregular primes is infinite' — irregularPrimes is defined in-file as the primes that are not regular; header cites Wikipedia 'Regular prime'.
Docstring: 'Conjecture: The set of regular primes is infinite' (RegularPrimeConjecture), from the Wikipedia 'Regular prime' article.
Header: M(n) equivalently counts 'the number of antichains (Sperner families) in the Boolean lattice'.
The statement quantifies over integer polynomials f : ℤ[X] satisfying the Bunyakovsky and Schinzel conditions.
The statement quantifies over non-constant rational polynomials p(x) in Q[x] with positive leading coefficient.
The question asks for a polynomial f in Z[X] of degree at least 2 whose positive values tile Z with a set A.
Sekanina [Sek59] showed no such complement A exists for the polynomial f(x) = X^2.
The variant conjectures no such complement A exists for the polynomial X^3.
The statement rules out quadratic polynomials f = aX^2 + bX + c (found by AlphaProof for X^2 - X + 1 and generalised).
The variant, asked in [Sek59], conjectures no complement A exists for the polynomial X^k for any k >= 2.
The problem concerns random polynomials f(z) with coefficients drawn uniformly from {-1,1} (Kac polynomials).
Erdos and Offord counted real roots of a random degree-n polynomial with plus-minus-1 coefficients.
Yakir proved almost all Kac polynomials have n/2 + O(n^(9/10)) roots in the unit disk.
The variant concerns random polynomials with coefficients drawn uniformly from {0,1}.

Module header: 'Why do polynomials with coefficients 0,1 like to have only factors with 0,1 coefficients?'; the statement is about monic real polynomials whose product is a 0,1 polynomial.

The **Juggler Conjecture** states that every juggler sequence (iterating jugglerStep from any positive integer) reaches 1; the file references the Wikipedia article Juggler sequence, whose Wikidata item is 'sequence hypothesized to always terminate at 1'.
Strong form asks f(n) < n^(c/log log n) for the same maximal-equidistant-points function f.
f(n) is maximal such that every point x in an n-point planar set has at least f(n) points 'equidistant from x'.
The core predicate HasNEquidistantProperty is about n vertices equidistant from a given vertex of the polygon.
Conjecture 1.5 bounds diam(A_n) for the alternating group on n elements by n^C.
Docstring: 'What is the largest product-free set in the alternating group A_n?'
'If $n \equiv 0 \pmod 4$, the Galois group of $f_n$ is isomorphic to the alternating group $A_n$'.
The question is whether the alternating series sum_{n=1}^infty (-1)^n n/p_n converges (Summable of a (-1)^(k+1)-signed sequence).
States there are infinitely many Steiner systems with t = 4, via Keevash's 2014 existence theorem.
States there are infinitely many Steiner systems with t = 5, settled nonconstructively by Keevash (2014).
Asks to construct an S(t,k,n) Steiner system with t > 5; no Steiner system with t > 5 is explicitly known, per the referenced Steiner system article and Epoch AI problem.
States existence of the Steiner system S(5,6,12), the small Witt design, one of only two known Steiner systems with t = 5.
States existence of the Steiner system S(5,8,24), the large Witt design constructed by Witt (1938).
A_k is defined as the limsup of |sum_j e(k x_j)| with e(x)=e^{2 pi i x}, i.e. the growth of exponential sums along the sequence.
The conjecture is entirely about nil ideals: 'the sum of two nil left ideals is nil', with IsNil defined as every element nilpotent.

Module header 'The 1/3–2/3 conjecture' cites the Wikipedia article; the decl asks whether every finite non-total poset has x, y appearing in order with probability between 1/3 and 2/3 in a random linear extension.
The hypothesis G.CliqueFree 4 forbids the complete graph K_4.
The constructed finite graph must satisfy G.CliqueFree 4, i.e. contain no K_4.
The pair in question is (completeGraph (Fin 4), completeGraph (Fin 3)), i.e. (K_4, K_3); this case is exactly Problem 595.
Folkman/Nesetril-Rodl: a K_4-free graph whose edges cannot be n-coloured without a monochromatic K_3 (completeGraph (Fin 4), completeGraph (Fin 3)).
The Erdős–Gyárfás conjecture colours 'the edges of K_{r^2+1}' and looks at the induced K_{r+1} on r+1 vertices.
The variant asks the analogous question for parallelograms of every area, defined via parallel sides.
The file references 'MathWorld, Harmonic Series'; the Borwein series is a harmonic-type series with sinusoidal coefficient (2/3 + 1/3 sin n)^n / n.
Chvátal's conjecture concerns a Decreasing (downward-closed) family of finite sets, i.e. an abstract simplicial complex, and its maximum intersecting subfamilies.
Docstring '**Agrawal's Primality Conjecture**' with header referencing Wikipedia Agrawal%27s_conjecture; states the (X-1)^n congruence implies n prime or n^2 = 1 mod r.
Docstring: Popovych's 2018 conjecture is 'a stronger version of Agrawal's conjecture', not the conjecture itself.
The Lam-Litt conjecture concerns power series solutions of algebraic ODEs (IsSolutionOfAlgebraicODE) with coefficients in Z[1/N] being algebraic.
The 3)=>2) implication of the Lam-Litt conjecture is stated for power series solutions of an algebraic ODE given by a rational function g.
De Giorgi's conjecture concerns bounded entire solutions of Δu + u - u^3 = 0, the (stationary) Allen-Cahn equation.
Setup: 'An almost-disjoint family is one in which pairwise intersections are finite'; the hypothesis is (A i ∩ A j).Finite for i ≠ j.
The witness sequence f n = n.fib -/+ 1 is built from Fibonacci numbers, per Graham's 'A property of Fibonacci numbers' [Gr64d].

The best-possible statement uses the same Fibonacci-defined constant c = 1 + sum 1/F_{2k}.
The Selfridge condition requires F_{p+1} = 0 mod p, a divisibility condition on Fibonacci numbers ((p+1).fib).
Docstring asks whether infinitely many n have all exponents distinct in the 'factorisation into distinct primes' of n(n+1).
Conditional theorem concludes distinct exponents in the prime factorization of n(n+1) via Set.InjOn factorization.

Problem asks for 'the largest subset of [N] with no solution to x + 3y = 2z + 2w in distinct integers', i.e. a set avoiding a linear equation (cf. Ruzsa, 'Solving a linear equation in a set of integers').
Maximising affine translates of {0,1,3} in a set of n integers is [Aa19] 'Maximising the number of solutions to a linear equation in a set of integers'.
Docstring: 'It follows from a slight strengthening of the Goldbach conjecture that every odd number can be written as n - phi(n)'; the Lean statement takes a Goldbach-style hypothesis.
Docstring asks whether every even integer greater than 2 is the sum of two primes, referenced to the Goldbach's conjecture Wikipedia page.
The problem is about Beurling generalized primes/integers (Erdos951Prop is stated via beurlingInteger and IsBeurlingPrimes), the subject covered by the Beurling zeta function article.
Docstring: 'Beurling conjectured that if the number of Beurling integers in [1,x] is x + o(log x), then a must be the sequence of primes'.
Knuth's 'Claude's Cycles' construction gives a Hamiltonian arc decomposition of the cube digraph for all odd m > 1.

Leinster's Theorem 2.1: an abelian group is a Leinster group iff it is cyclic of perfect number order.

Open conjecture asking whether there are infinitely many Leinster groups, defined per the referenced Wikipedia article as groups whose normal subgroup orders sum to twice the group order.

Asks whether every integer is 'the sum of a prime and at most k powers of 2' (p.Prime in sumPrimeAndTwoPows).
Docstring asks 'Are there infinitely many primes in each class?' for the class-r classification of primes p by prime factors of p+1.

Docstring asks whether there are infinitely many primes p with p = 2^k 3^l q + 1 for some prime q.

The problem asks whether there are infinitely many n > 2 such that n - 2^k is prime for all k >= 1 with 2^k < n.

p_n is the smallest prime ≡ 1 (mod n); the question compares m_n with p_n for almost all n.

The set Erdos9A consists of odd numbers not expressible as 'a prime plus two powers of 2'.

Erdos was unable to prove 'there must exist a prime between n_1 and n_2' when the two products share prime factors.

k(n) is the maximal k such that some m <= n has m+1,...,m+k 'each divisible by a prime > k'.
The docstring defines u n = p_n/n where p_n is the nth prime and asks about the density of n with u_n < u_{n+1}.

The statement quantifies over primes p and asks for a prime q that is a primitive root modulo p.
The sequence in question is {alpha p_n} where p_n is the sequence of primes (Nat.nth Nat.Prime).
The iterated differences d start from d^0(n) = p_n, the sequence of prime numbers.
The statement asserts every even n > 2 is a sum of two primes.
The docstring lets p be a large prime and A be the set of all primes less than p (Erdos-Odlyzko-Sarkozy 1987).
The exponent p in x^2 - 2 = y^p ranges over odd primes (hypotheses hp : p.Prime, hodd : Odd p).
A228828: numbers n such that n^2 + pi(n) is prime; the conjecture asserts infinitely many such n.
A231201 (Zhi-Wei Sun): every n>1 can be written n = x+y with x,y>0 such that 2^x + y is prime.

The conjecture quantifies over every prime p and its primitive roots.
Docstring: for every n > 1 there exists k < n such that nk + 1 is a prime (A34693 is the smallest such k).
Conjecture: for every member k other than 8, k + 1 is prime — the docstring notes the sequence's strong connection to primes.
Wagstaff's conjecture that there are infinitely many primes of the form 2^n + 2^i + 1 (primes with exactly three one bits).
Docstring '**Büchi's problem**: There exists a positive integer M such that...' states exactly the problem of the file's Wikipedia reference Büchi's_problem.

Docstring: the first widely studied open case, whether 196 is a base-10 Lychrel number.

Open question whether there are no Lychrel numbers in base 10, i.e. every positive integer eventually becomes a palindrome under n -> n + rev10(n); references the Lychrel number Wikipedia page and OEIS A023108.
The Krenn-Gu N=4 question asks for non-existence of weightings whose perfect-matching sums (pmSumN) satisfy EqSystemN over C.
The equation system is a weighted sum over perfect matchings of K_N (pmSumN), per the header 'weighted sums over perfect matchings'.
